title: SA Inflation Tracker with Interactive Filters
text: South Africa’s CPI is broken into several spending categories — food, housing, transport, and more. This post builds an interactive tracker so you can explore how different categories have moved over time. Use the dropdown to switch categories.

title: SA Inflation Tracker with Interactive Filters
text: South Africa’s CPI is broken into several spending categories — food, housing, transport, and more. This post builds an interactive tracker so you can explore how different categories have moved over time. Use the dropdown to switch categories.

title: SA Inflation Tracker with Interactive Filters
text:
Setup


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
import plotly.express as px

title: SA Inflation Tracker with Interactive Filters
text:
Simulated CPI Data


Code
np.random.seed(42)

months = pd.date_range("2018-01-01", "2024-12-01", freq="MS")
categories = ["Food & Non-Alcoholic Beverages", "Housing & Utilities",
              "Transport", "Education", "Health", "Clothing & Footwear",
              "Miscellaneous"]

# Each category gets a different base inflation trend
base_rates = {
    "Food & Non-Alcoholic Beverages": 0.007,
    "Housing & Utilities":            0.005,
    "Transport":                      0.006,
    "Education":                      0.004,
    "Health":                         0.005,
    "Clothing & Footwear":            0.003,
    "Miscellaneous":                  0.004
}

rows = []
for cat in categories:
    index = 100.0
    for m in months:
        index *= (1 + base_rates[cat] + np.random.normal(0, 0.002))
        rows.append({"date": m, "category": cat, "index": round(index, 2)})

df = pd.DataFrame(rows)

# Compute year-on-year % change
df = df.sort_values(["category", "date"])
df["yoy_pct"] = df.groupby("category")["index"].pct_change(12) * 100
df["yoy_pct"] = df["yoy_pct"].round(2)
df.head()









date
category
index
yoy_pct

title: SA Inflation Tracker with Interactive Filters
section: Interactive Chart: Year-on-Year Inflation by Category
text:
Interactive Chart: Year-on-Year Inflation by Category


Code
fig = go.Figure()

first = True
for cat in categories:
    subset = df[df["category"] == cat].dropna(subset=["yoy_pct"])
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=subset["date"],
        y=subset["yoy_pct"],
        name=cat,
        visible=True if first else False,
        mode="lines+markers",
        marker=dict(size=4),
        hovertemplate="%{x|%b %Y}<br>YoY: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>"
    ))
    first = False

# Build dropdown buttons — one per category
buttons = []
for i, cat in enumerate(categories):
    visibility = [j == i for j in range(len(categories))]
    buttons.append(dict(
        label=cat,
        method="update",
        args=[{"visible": visibility},
              {"title": f"YoY CPI Inflation — {cat}"}]
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title=f"YoY CPI Inflation — {categories[0]}",
    xaxis_title="",
    yaxis_title="Year-on-Year % Change",
    updatemenus=[dict(
        buttons=buttons,
        direction="down",
        showactive=True,
        x=0.01, xanchor="left",
        y=1.15, yanchor="top"
    )],
    height=450
)

fig.show()




                            
                                            


Figure 1: Year-on-year CPI inflation — use the dropdown to change category

title: SA Inflation Tracker with Interactive Filters
text:
All Categories: Heatmap Overview


Code
df["year"] = df["date"].dt.year
annual = (df.dropna(subset=["yoy_pct"])
          .groupby(["category", "year"])["yoy_pct"]
          .mean()
          .reset_index())

pivot = annual.pivot(index="category", columns="year", values="yoy_pct").round(1)

fig2 = px.imshow(
    pivot,
    text_auto=True,
    color_continuous_scale="RdYlGn_r",
    title="Average Annual YoY CPI Inflation by Category (%)",
    labels=dict(x="Year", y="", color="YoY %")
)
fig2.update_layout(height=400)
fig2.show()




                            
                                            


Figure 2: Annual average YoY inflation across all categories

title: SA Inflation Tracker with Interactive Filters
text:
Key Points

Food and Transport show the highest inflation volatility
Education costs have risen steadily with little variation
The post-2022 global inflation pulse is visible across all categories







Note



All data is simulated for illustration. In a production post, replace with actual Stats SA CPI series data downloadable from statssa.gov.za.

title: South Africa’s Load Shedding: A Data Story
text: Load shedding has become a defining feature of life in South Africa. In this post I use R to build a clear picture of how shedding frequency and severity changed over time, using publicly available Eskom stage data.

title: South Africa’s Load Shedding: A Data Story
text: Load shedding has become a defining feature of life in South Africa. In this post I use R to build a clear picture of how shedding frequency and severity changed over time, using publicly available Eskom stage data.

title: South Africa’s Load Shedding: A Data Story
text:
Setup


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(scales)

title: South Africa’s Load Shedding: A Data Story
text:
Simulated Data
For this example we simulate a plausible load shedding dataset. In a real post you would load actual Eskom data here (e.g. from an API or a downloaded CSV).


Code
set.seed(42)

# Simulate daily load shedding stages across 2022–2024
dates <- seq(as.Date("2022-01-01"), as.Date("2024-12-31"), by = "day")

stage <- ifelse(
  dates < as.Date("2022-06-01"),
  sample(0:2, length(dates), replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2)),
  ifelse(
    dates < as.Date("2023-06-01"),
    sample(0:6, length(dates), replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.15, 0.1, 0.05)),
    sample(0:4, length(dates), replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.3, 0.25, 0.2, 0.15, 0.1))
  )
)

df <- tibble(date = dates, stage = stage)

head(df)


# A tibble: 6 × 2
  date       stage
  <date>     <int>
1 2022-01-01     2
2 2022-01-02     2
3 2022-01-03     0
4 2022-01-04     2
5 2022-01-05     1
6 2022-01-06     1

title: South Africa’s Load Shedding: A Data Story
text:
Monthly Average Stage


Code
df_monthly <- df |>
  mutate(month = floor_date(date, "month")) |>
  group_by(month) |>
  summarise(avg_stage = mean(stage), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(df_monthly, aes(x = month, y = avg_stage, fill = avg_stage)) +
  geom_col(width = 25) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#ffe599", high = "#cc0000", name = "Avg Stage") +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "3 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:6) +
  labs(
    title    = "Monthly Average Load Shedding Stage",
    subtitle = "South Africa · Simulated data for illustration",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Average Stage"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))








Figure 1: Average daily load shedding stage by month

title: South Africa’s Load Shedding: A Data Story
section: Days at Each Stage Per Year
text:
Days at Each Stage Per Year


Code
df |>
  mutate(year = year(date)) |>
  count(year, stage) |>
  mutate(stage = factor(stage)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = stage, y = factor(year), fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#fff7bc", high = "#d7191c", name = "Days") +
  labs(
    title = "Days Spent at Each Load Shedding Stage",
    x     = "Stage",
    y     = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)








Figure 2: Distribution of load shedding stages per year

title: South Africa’s Load Shedding: A Data Story
text:
Key Observations

2022 saw a dramatic escalation into higher stages from mid-year
2023 was the worst year on record, with frequent Stage 4–6 events
2024 showed tentative improvement, with more Stage 0 days returning







Note



This post uses simulated data for illustration purposes. Replace the data block with real Eskom data for a production analysis.

title: SA Housing Market: Interactive Price Trends
text: This post explores simulated residential property price indices across four major South African metros. The charts here are fully interactive — hover to see values, click the legend to toggle metros, and zoom in on any period.

title: SA Housing Market: Interactive Price Trends
text: This post explores simulated residential property price indices across four major South African metros. The charts here are fully interactive — hover to see values, click the legend to toggle metros, and zoom in on any period.

title: SA Housing Market: Interactive Price Trends
text:
Setup


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(plotly)
library(scales)

title: SA Housing Market: Interactive Price Trends
SA Housing Market: Interactive Price Trends
text:
Interactive Price Index Chart


Code
plot_ly(
  df,
  x     = ~date,
  y     = ~round(index, 1),
  color = ~metro,
  type  = "scatter",
  mode  = "lines",
  hovertemplate = "<b>%{fullData.name}</b><br>%{x|%b %Y}<br>Index: %{y}<extra></extra>"
) |>
  layout(
    title   = list(text = "SA Residential Property Price Index (Base = 100, Q1 2015)"),
    xaxis   = list(title = ""),
    yaxis   = list(title = "Price Index"),
    legend  = list(title = list(text = "Metro")),
    hovermode = "x unified"
  )








Figure 1: Residential property price index by metro (hover to explore)

title: SA Housing Market: Interactive Price Trends
text:
Interactive Bar: Latest Index Values


Code
latest <- df |>
  group_by(metro) |>
  slice_max(date, n = 1)

plot_ly(
  latest,
  x    = ~reorder(metro, index),
  y    = ~round(index, 1),
  type = "bar",
  color = ~metro,
  text = ~paste0(round(index, 1)),
  textposition = "outside",
  hovertemplate = "<b>%{x}</b><br>Index: %{y}<extra></extra>"
) |>
  layout(
    title  = "Latest Residential Price Index by Metro",
    xaxis  = list(title = ""),
    yaxis  = list(title = "Index Value"),
    showlegend = FALSE
  )








Figure 2: Latest index value per metro

title: SA Housing Market: Interactive Price Trends
text:
Takeaways

Cape Town has seen the strongest cumulative price growth since 2015
Durban has lagged significantly, with the lowest current index
All metros show post-2020 acceleration followed by softening into 2024







TipInteract with the charts



Click a city name in the legend to hide/show it. Drag on the chart to zoom in. Double-click to reset the view.
